1:
“Now before the feast of the passover, when Jesus knew that his hour was come that he should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved his own which were in the world, he loved them unto the end.” The witnesses read this verse as the solemn overture to the Passion narrative, and dwell on the love it names. The Latin Augustine ponders the phrase “unto the end” (eis telos): it may mean that Christ “loved His own up to His death,” yet, since his love “is not ended by death,” it rather means “He loved His own to death — i.e., His love for them led Him to death,” and so to himself, “for Christ is the end of the law” (Romans 10:4). The Eastern Chrysostom turns at once to the lesson: as Paul bids us “be imitators of me, as I also am of Christ” (1 Corinthians 11:1), so Christ “taught us virtue… not by words alone, but by actions,” the foot-washing being meekness made visible (Matthew 11:29).

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the hour and the objects of this love. Ellicott notes that Jesus, who had so often said his hour was “not yet come” (John 2:4; 7:6), “now knows with equal certainty that the hour is at hand that He should depart unto the Father.” “His own” are the believers whom he had made sons of God; he “loved them unto the end,” which Barnes takes of constancy — “he continued the proofs of his love until he was taken away by death.” On the timing, the commentators differ: Robertson and most place the scene at the Last Supper, “our Thursday evening,” the night before the crucifixion, while Gill and Lightfoot, idiosyncratically, set it earlier at Bethany. Henry grants the chronology is debated but the substance plain.

Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the verse’s comfort. John passes by what the others had told, to explain that Christ “testified, by this visible sign, that the love with which he embraced them was firm and lasting,” so that, “though they were deprived of his presence, they might still be convinced that death itself would not quench this love.” The witnesses thus find in this opening line the key to the whole chapter, indeed to the Passion: that all Christ is about to do — the washing of the feet, the giving of his body, the going to the cross — flows from a love for his own that, knowing the hour of departure, loves them “to the end,” to the uttermost, and to the death. The greatness of the love is measured by the lowliness and the suffering to which it stoops.
2:
“And supper being ended (rather, during supper), the devil having now put into the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, to betray him.” The witnesses correct the translation and weigh the devil’s work. The grammatical-historical commentators agree that the rendering “supper being ended” is wrong: Robertson reads the present participle (deipnou ginomenou) as a genitive absolute, “during supper,” for “verse 4 shows plainly that the meal was still going on,” and Ellicott prefers “it now becoming supper time,” since the supper was “not ended” (vv.12, 26) but they “had already reclined.” On the betrayal, the commentators note that the devil “put it into the heart” of Judas — Barnes glosses “cast it into the heart” (cf. Luke 22:3; Acts 5:3) — and Robertson observes that John’s “already” (ēdē) is pertinent, since Luke says Satan entered Judas when he offered to betray, and Jesus had a year before seen that Judas “was a devil” (John 6:70).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out why John notes the betrayal here. The Evangelist mentions that this was done “while Judas already resolved to betray Christ,” both “to show the wonderful patience of Christ, who could endure to wash the feet of such a wicked and detestable traitor,” and because Christ “purposely selected the time when he was near death, for performing… the last act of his life.” The witnesses thus find the verse setting the foot-washing against its darkest backdrop: at the very table where the Lord stoops to serve, the traitor reclines with the plot already lodged in his heart by the devil. The love that loves “to the end” will, in the next verses, wash even the feet of the man who is about to sell him — so that the patience and humility of Christ are displayed not toward friends only but toward the betrayer himself.

The witnesses note the deliberate juxtaposition John draws. At the same table sit the Lord who is about to humble himself in love and the disciple in whom the devil has already lodged the plot of betrayal. The Evangelist sets the two side by side so that the depth of Christ’s humility may be felt: he will kneel before Judas as before the rest, knowing what is in his heart. The foot-washing is thus framed from the start as an act of love that reaches even to the man who is selling him.
3:
“Jesus knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he was come from God, and went to God.” The witnesses read this as the deliberate setting of Christ’s supreme dignity against the lowly act that follows. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the paradox: with “the full consciousness of His supreme power and divine origin” (Ellicott), Jesus stoops to a slave’s task — “the dignity of his nature… shows how low he would stoop to teach and save his people” (Barnes). Robertson notes the participle “knowing” (eidōs), repeated from v.1, “accenting the full consciousness of Jesus,” and the threefold claim: all things given into his hands (cf. Matthew 28:18; Matthew 11:27), come from God, and going to God (John 16:28). Ellicott sets the verse expressly against the great kenosis text: “because He was conscious of all this, He left the disciples an example of the self-denial which is the necessary outcome of love” — for he who “subsisting in the form of God… emptied Himself by taking… the form of a servant” (Philippians 2:6–7).

Clarke draws out the pastoral aim: knowing himself “almost at the end of his race,” Christ “thought it necessary to leave them a lesson of humility exemplified by himself, to deliver them from the bad influence of those false ideas” of a worldly kingdom which their ambition had so often betrayed. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the verse the very logic of the gospel: it is not in ignorance of his greatness but in full knowledge of it that Christ humbles himself. The witnesses thus read the clause as the hinge of the scene: the One into whose hands all things are given, who came from God and returns to God, girds himself with a towel — so that the depth of the condescension is measured by the height from which it stoops. The Lord of all becomes the servant of all, that his disciples may learn the humility that love requires.

The witnesses draw the abiding lesson that true greatness is shown in lowly service. It is precisely the One who knows himself Lord of all who is free to take the lowest place; the consciousness of his dignity does not hinder but enables his humility. So the verse rebukes the disciples’ rivalry over precedence, and teaches the church that the higher a man truly stands in God, the lower he may safely stoop in love — for the towel in the hands of the Son of God is the surest measure of what greatness means.
4:
“He riseth from supper, and laid aside his garments; and took a towel, and girded himself.” The witnesses dwell on the deliberate, servile preparation. The grammatical-historical commentators note each step as the action of one taking a slave’s part: Christ “laid aside” his outer garments (the loose robes that would hinder the work) and “girded himself” with a towel, exactly as a servant would prepare to wash the guests’ feet (Gill, Barnes, Robertson). Robertson observes the verbs are chosen to picture the menial office; Ellicott marks that the rising from the table and the disrobing are told with a fulness that fixes attention on the strangeness of the Master doing the servant’s work. The towel girded about him is the badge of the slave, assumed by the Lord of glory.

The witnesses are quick to draw the symbol the fathers and Reformers found in it. The “laying aside” of his garments, several note, is a fitting image of the great humbling of v.3 — he who would lay down his life first lays aside his robe; he who “emptied himself” (Philippians 2:7) here visibly empties his hands of dignity to take up the towel. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the whole gesture as the acted parable of the incarnation: the Son, putting off the visible tokens of his majesty, clothes himself in the form of a servant. The witnesses thus find in this verse no mere preliminary but a deliberate enactment: every motion — rising, disrobing, girding — is calculated to teach, and to prefigure, the deeper self-abasement of the cross, where the Lord who girded himself with a towel will be stripped and lifted up for the salvation of the very men whose feet he now prepares to wash.

The witnesses note that John lingers over each motion deliberately, as though to fix the scene forever in the church’s memory. The rising, the laid-aside garments, the girding with the towel — these are the gestures of a slave, and the Evangelist records them one by one because it is the Master who performs them. Every detail heightens the wonder, and prepares the disciples (and the reader) for the searching question of the act: if the Lord does this, what then ought his servants to do?
5:
“After that he poureth water into a bason, and began to wash the disciples’ feet, and to wipe them with the towel wherewith he was girded.” The witnesses fasten on the astonishing reality of the act — the Lord performing the lowest of servile offices. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the washing of feet was “a peculiar action” reserved for the meanest slaves (Gill), since guests came with sandalled, dusty feet, and a host might provide water for the feet to be washed (Barnes, cf. Luke 7:44). Robertson notes the word for “bason” (niptēr) and the menial nature of the task; Clarke recalls that such washing was usually done before a meal. That Jesus himself “poureth” the water and stoops to “wash” and “wipe” each disciple’s feet is, all agree, an inversion of every expectation — the Teacher and Lord doing what no disciple would have stooped to do for another.

The witnesses read the act on two levels. Literally, it is the supreme lesson in humility, which Christ will press home in vv.14–15: if he, the Lord, washes their feet, they ought to wash one another’s. Figuratively, the fathers and Reformers find in it a picture of cleansing — the daily washing of the feet, soiled by the world’s dust, which the believer needs even after the bath of regeneration (a theme Christ himself will open in v.10). Calvin, for the Reformed, marks how the whole transaction magnifies the love of v.1: this is love that does not merely speak but kneels, taking the basin and the towel. The witnesses thus find in the foot-washing the heart of the chapter enacted: the One into whose hands the Father has given all things kneels before twelve men — including his betrayer — to do a slave’s work, that he might leave his church an unforgettable image of the love that stoops to serve.

The witnesses observe, too, that he begins to wash the feet of all alike, making no exception even of Judas. The basin and the towel pass from disciple to disciple without distinction, the same patient love kneeling before the faithful and the false. In this the foot-washing becomes more than a lesson in humility; it is a picture of the love that serves its enemies, and a silent appeal to the conscience of the betrayer, whose feet the Lord he is about to sell now takes tenderly into his hands.
6:
“Then cometh he to Simon Peter: and Peter saith unto him, Lord, dost thou wash my feet?” The witnesses dwell on Peter’s startled protest and debate the order of washing. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic position of the pronouns — “Dost thou my feet wash?” (Robertson) — and that “Peter, we may suppose, drew his feet up, as he spoke, in his impulsive humility.” On whether Peter was first or came later, the commentators are unsure: Ellicott calls it “a point of no importance,” though the natural impression is that “Peter’s turn came after that of at least one other.” The fathers read the order symbolically. Origen pictures Christ as a physician who “begins with those who want his attention most,” so he washes “the most unclean” first and “comes at last to Peter, who needed the washing less than any.” Theophylact and Chrysostom suggest he “washed the traitor first, and came next to Peter,” the other disciples being “checked by the reply to Peter” — their silence a sign “not of want of love, but of reverence.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, enters into Peter’s fear: “who would not be filled with fear at having his feet washed by the Son of God?” Though “it was a piece of the greatest audacity for the servant to contradict his Lord,” Peter “preferred doing this to the suffering of his feet to be washed by his Lord and God.” Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs the protest more critically: it “expresses strong dislike of the action as foolish and unsuitable,” and though “the modesty would be worthy of commendation,” it forgets that “obedience is of greater value in the sight of God than any kind of honour or service.” The witnesses thus find in Peter’s “Lord, dost thou wash my feet?” the first stirring of a reverence that is real but unsubmissive — a humility that, by refusing what Christ commands, becomes its own kind of pride, and that Christ will at once correct.

The witnesses note that Peter’s instinct, though wrong, springs from a true sense of the gulf between the Lord and himself. He cannot bear that the One he knows to be his Master and God should kneel at his feet; the very depth of his reverence makes the service seem intolerable. Yet, as the next verses show, such reverence must learn to yield, for the love that stoops to wash will not be turned aside by a humility that refuses to be served.
7:
“Jesus answered and said unto him, What I do thou knowest not now; but thou shalt know hereafter.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s call to obedience before understanding. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic pronouns repeated from Peter’s protest — “I… thou” — by which Christ gently rebukes him (Ellicott): Peter’s words had almost implied that the Lord “knew not what he was doing,” when “the opposite was really the case.” The “hereafter” (meta tauta, “after these things”) refers, Barnes and Ellicott agree, chiefly to the explanation Christ will give at once (vv.14–17), the symbolical act being made unforgettable by being first performed and then expounded; Robertson notes the verb “thou shalt understand” (gnōsē) means “to know by experience,” “even if slowly.” Clarke paraphrases the sense: “Permit me to do it now, and I will shortly explain.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the principle the words enshrine: “we ought simply to obey Christ, even though we should not perceive the reason why he wishes this or that thing to be done.” As in “a well-regulated house” the head “has the sole right to say what ought to be done,” so “that man is too haughty, who refuses to obey the command of God, because he does not know the reason of it.” Wesley applies it to the believer’s walk: “we do not now know perfectly any of his works… it is enough that we can love and obey now, and that we shall know hereafter.” The witnesses thus find in this verse a charter of faith’s obedience: the disciple is not to wait until he comprehends Christ’s purpose before submitting to his will, but to trust the Master’s wisdom and yield, assured that the meaning, hidden now, will be made plain in its time. Peter’s impatience to understand must give way to the humility that obeys.

The witnesses add that the verse teaches a settled posture for the whole Christian life. Much of what God does with his people is, at the time, dark to them — afflictions, delays, denials — and only afterward is its purpose seen. To insist on understanding before submitting is to reverse the order of faith. The disciple is called rather to trust the wisdom and love of his Lord in the moment of incomprehension, sure that what is hidden now will be made plain hereafter.
8:
“Peter saith unto him, Thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus answered him, If I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me.” The witnesses note that Peter’s reverence has now hardened into obstinacy, and that Christ’s reply lifts the act to its spiritual meaning. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the vehemence of the refusal — Robertson’s “strong double negative… for ever” — and the searching answer. “The first condition of discipleship is self-surrender” (Westcott, in Robertson); Peter’s act “amounted to irreverence and want of confidence.” Barnes: “we should yield to all the plain and positive requirements of God, even if we cannot now see how obedience would promote his glory.” Ellicott hears in the “never” the very voice of “the loving, impulsive, but self-confident Peter of the earlier Gospels.” On “thou hast no part with me,” Clarke and Wesley find the deeper sense: the washing is “emblematical of something spiritual,” so that, Wesley says, “if I do not wash thee in my blood, and purify thee by my Spirit, thou canst have no communion with me, nor any share in the blessings of my kingdom.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, is sharp with Peter: “hitherto Peter’s modesty was excusable… but now he errs more grievously, when he has been corrected, and yet does not yield,” for “it is a common fault, that ignorance is closely followed by obstinacy.” The plea of reverence cannot excuse disobedience: “since he does not absolutely obey the injunction, the very desire of showing his respect for Christ” is at fault. The witnesses thus find the verse turning on a great gospel principle: to refuse the cleansing Christ offers, even out of misplaced humility, is to refuse him altogether. The same self-will that will not be served by Christ cannot be saved by him; only those who submit to be washed by him — who let him do for them what they cannot do for themselves — have part with him. Peter’s threatened loss of all is the warning to every soul that would dictate to its Lord the terms of its own cleansing.

The witnesses underline that this is the turning-point of the scene. Peter’s flat refusal forces the issue from courtesy into salvation: to be washed by Christ is no longer a question of etiquette but of belonging to him. The word ‘no part with me’ strips away all pretence of reverence and lays bare the true alternative — submit to be cleansed, or forfeit communion with the Lord. So the foot-washing, begun as a lesson in humility, becomes a parable of the gospel itself: there is no fellowship with Christ for those who will not let him do for them what they cannot do for themselves.
9:
“Simon Peter saith unto him, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.” The witnesses smile at Peter’s sudden swing from refusal to excess. The grammatical-historical commentators note the same impulsive temper now reversed: convinced that everything depends on this washing, and fearing to be “deprived of communion” with Christ (Gill), Peter wishes “to be cleansed entirely, thoroughly” — “not the feet only, but the whole man” (Ellicott). Robertson quotes Dods’ apt remark: “a moment ago he told his Master He was doing too much: now he tells Him He is doing too little.” Barnes reads his ardour as half-grasping the spiritual meaning — “his whole salvation, the entire question of his attachment to his Master, was involved” — so he asks for hands and head as well.

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks both the yielding and the error. When Peter heard “that he was ruined, if he did not accept the cleansing,” this “necessity proved… a sufficient instructor to tame him,” and “he therefore lays aside opposition and yields.” Yet “here too he goes wrong through thoughtlessness,” treating “as a thing of no value the benefit which he had already received,” as if he were “altogether covered with pollution” and needed a wholly new cleansing. Wesley sums it tenderly: “how fain would man be wiser than God! Yet this was well meant, though ignorant earnestness.” The witnesses thus find in Peter a portrait of the believer who, corrected, overcorrects — moving from refusing Christ’s grace to demanding more of it than is needed, and in both betraying that he does not yet understand. His error opens the way for the great word of the next verse, in which Christ distinguishes the one cleansing that makes wholly clean from the daily washing the cleansed still need.

The witnesses find in Peter’s extravagance a lovable but instructive fault. He would rather have too much of Christ than too little, and his ‘hands and head’ betray a heart that cannot bear to be parted from his Lord. Yet zeal without knowledge still misreads the Master’s mind, asking for what is not needed because it has not understood what is given. The verse thus prepares the gentle correction of v.10, where Christ measures out exactly how much washing the cleansed disciple requires.
10:
“Jesus saith to him, He that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet, but is clean every whit: and ye are clean, but not all.” The witnesses find here a great distinction between the once-for-all cleansing and the daily washing the believer still needs. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the two different verbs: “he that is washed” (leloumenos, has bathed the whole body) “needeth not save to wash (nipsasthai) his feet” (Robertson, Ellicott). The bathed man, coming from the bath to the table, has only contracted dust on his feet by the way; he does not need another full washing, but only the cleansing of what the walk has soiled. Gill reads the bath as regeneration and justification — washed “in the blood of Christ… clean every whit,” “justified from all things,” not by baptism but by Christ — while the feet are the daily defilements of life that need continual cleansing. Barnes calls it “a difficult passage,” but agrees the sense distinguishes the man already cleansed from the daily renewal he requires.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, fastens on the dark exception — “and ye are clean, but not all” — and notes that the Evangelist himself explains it (v.11): “He knew who it was that should betray Him.” The witnesses thus read the verse as both comfort and warning. For the believer it is comfort: he who is once truly washed by Christ — born again, justified — is “clean every whit,” and needs not to be regenerated afresh each day, but only to bring his soiled feet, the sins of daily walk, to be cleansed by the same Lord. But the closing words sound the warning: among the very company whose feet Christ washes, one is not clean, for outward washing cannot cleanse a heart that harbours betrayal. The witnesses thus find in this verse the gospel’s balance — assurance for the cleansed, who need not despair at daily failure, and solemn caution that no ritual or nearness to Christ avails where the heart is unrenewed.

The witnesses draw the doctrine the verse has long taught the church. Justification and regeneration are not repeated; the soul once washed by Christ is wholly clean before God, and does not return each day to be born again. But the believer, walking through a defiling world, daily contracts the dust of sin upon his feet, and must come continually to his Lord for the cleansing of confession and renewed grace. So the verse guards against two errors at once — the despair that fears it must be saved afresh after every fall, and the presumption that, once cleansed, it needs no daily washing at all.
11:
“For he knew who should betray him; therefore said he, Ye are not all clean.” The witnesses take this as the Evangelist’s own explanation of the ominous exception in v.10. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the “not all” points to Judas, whose feet Christ had washed with the rest, but whose heart, harbouring the betrayal already lodged there by the devil (v.2), was unclean. Barnes observes that Judas, “though his feet were washed,” was “not clean in heart”; the outward washing reached the body but not the soul. Gill reads it of the want of regeneration — Judas was never truly “washed” in the deeper sense, never born again or cleansed in the blood of Christ — so that his presence among the disciples and his share in the foot-washing left him as defiled within as ever. Ellicott and Robertson note how John, writing long after, draws back the veil to show that Christ knew the traitor from the first.

The witnesses draw the searching lesson. The verse stands as a solemn proof that the nearest place to Christ — at his table, under his hands, among his chosen — cannot of itself cleanse the heart. Judas received the same outward grace as Peter and John, yet remained unclean, because the inner washing of the Spirit was never his. The witnesses thus read it as a perpetual warning to the church: that one may be reckoned among the disciples, partake of the holiest ordinances, and even have his feet washed by the Lord himself, and yet, for want of a renewed heart, have no part with him. The “not all” of this verse is the shadow that falls across the whole scene — the reminder that, even at the supper of love, a betrayer sits clean of foot but foul of soul, knowing and known.

The witnesses observe how the shadow of Judas lies across the whole foot-washing. Christ kneels before him, takes his feet into his hands, and washes them as tenderly as the rest, yet knows that this man’s heart is already sold. The outward act of love is not withheld even from the betrayer; but neither does it cleanse him, for the washing that avails is inward and of the Spirit. The verse thus leaves the reader with a sobering picture — the same basin, the same towel, the same Lord, and yet one whose feet are clean and whose soul is not.
12:
“So after he had washed their feet, and had taken his garments, and was set down again, he said unto them, Know ye what I have done to you?” The witnesses note that Christ now turns from act to explanation, fulfilling his promise that Peter should “know hereafter” (v.7). The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the question. Robertson reads “was set down again” (anepesen, reclined) and calls “Know ye what I have done to you?” a “searching question, particularly to Simon Peter and Judas.” Clarke and Origen note it may be read as imperative as well as interrogative — “Consider what I have done” — to rouse their minds and direct them to the meaning. Ellicott agrees the question is asked “not to be answered, but to direct their attention… to the interpretation which follows.” Gill marks the “each of them” in “after he had washed their feet,” the service performed to all the twelve, Judas included.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, sees Christ “mindful of His promise to Peter” now beginning “to teach him,” and Alcuin finds a mystery even in his taking his garments again — an image of the risen Lord resuming his glory after the humiliation. Calvin, for the Reformed, states the purpose plainly: Christ “discloses the reason of what he had done; namely, that he wishes to be followed by all the godly, that none might grudge to descend to do a service to his brethren and equals, however mean and low that service might be.” The witnesses thus find the verse the hinge of the scene: the act is finished, and now its lesson is drawn out. The Lord who has stooped to wash resumes his place and his garments, and asks his disciples whether they have grasped what he meant — preparing to make the foot-washing the permanent pattern of humble love among his people.

The witnesses note the deliberate teaching method. Christ does not merely command humility; he first performs it, then explains it, so that the lesson is fixed forever in the memory by sight before it is pressed home in words. The question ‘know ye what I have done?’ invites the disciples to interpret the act for themselves, and prepares them to receive the rule he is about to lay down — that what the Lord has done to them, they must do to one another.
13:
“Ye call me Master and Lord: and ye say well; for so I am.” The witnesses note that Christ accepts the disciples’ titles before drawing his lesson from them. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the two words: “Master” means Teacher (not the Hebrew “Rabbi” here, Ellicott), and “Lord” the reverence due to a sovereign and ruler (Barnes; cf. Matthew 23:8–10). Gill and Lightfoot note that “Rabbi” and “Mar” were the honoured titles the Jewish pupils gave their doctors, given by the disciples to Christ “not out of flattery, but reverence”; Clarke recalls the double title “Rabbi vemore” reserved for the most accredited teachers. Robertson marks the emphatic “Ye” and the verb “call” (phōneite, address with a title), and the titular nominative “the Master… the Lord,” comparable to Thomas’s “my Lord and my God” (John 20:28).

The point the witnesses draw is the force of Christ’s frank acceptance: “ye say well; for so I am.” He does not disclaim the dignity but confirms it — “he was their Teacher and Instructor, and… their Sovereign and King” (Barnes). And it is precisely on this acknowledged greatness that the coming exhortation will rest: the higher his rank, the more his stooping binds them. The witnesses thus find in the verse the necessary premise of the argument: Christ first establishes that he is indeed their Master and Lord, no mere equal performing a courtesy, so that when he reminds them in the next verse that he has washed their feet, the lesson lands with full weight. The condescension is not that of a fellow-servant but of the Lord himself; and it is that very Lordship which makes his example so commanding a rule of humility for those who own him.

The witnesses also note that Christ’s acceptance of the titles guards against a false humility that would deny his dignity. He does not pretend to be less than he is in order to serve; rather, being truly Lord and Master, he serves. This is the very glory of his condescension, and the pattern for his people: not to disown one’s station, but, whatever it is, to use it in love for others. The greatness is real, and the stooping is real, and the two together make the lesson.
14:
“If I then, your Lord and Master, have washed your feet; ye also ought to wash one another’s feet.” The witnesses draw the lesson and debate whether it enjoins a literal rite. The grammatical-historical commentators read the argument as one “from the greater to the less” (Calvin): if the Lord himself stoops so low, much more must the disciples humble themselves for one another (Ellicott). Robertson notes the first-class condition (“if I, being what I am, washed your feet, as I did”) and the verb “ought” (opheilete, owe a debt) — “the obligation rests on you” to do “this or any other needed service.” On the question of a literal observance, Barnes argues at length that it is not instituted as a religious rite like baptism or the Supper: “it was a rite of hospitality among the Jews, a common, well-known thing, performed by servants,” and “it is the manifest design of his command” to teach humble service, not a ceremony. Wesley presses the same point with a telling question: “why do we not read of any one apostle ever washing the feet of any other? Because they understood the Lord better… he never designed that this should be literally taken,” but “to teach the great lesson of humble love.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, drives home the moral: “pride hinders us from maintaining that equality which ought to exist amongst us,” but Christ, “far exalted above all others, stoops down, that he may make the proud men ashamed,” who think themselves “not bound to hold intercourse with the brethren.” The witnesses thus find the verse commanding not a ceremony but a disposition — the readiness to perform for one another even the meanest offices of love. The literal act is the parable; the thing required is the lowly, mutual service of which it is the picture. If the Lord and Master has washed the feet of his servants, no follower of his may count any service too low, or any brother beneath his help; the towel passes from Christ’s hands to theirs, that they may do for each other what he has done for them.
15:
“For I have given you an example, that ye should do as I have done to you.” The witnesses define the kind of imitation Christ requires. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the example lies “in the principle, not in the specific act” (Ellicott): “it is not ‘that which I have done to you,’ but ‘according as I have done to you’” — the imitation “to be worked out in applying the same principle of love and self-sacrifice in all the varying circumstances of life.” Robertson notes the word “example” (hypodeigma, an illustration set before the eyes), and that Peter, with this incident in mind, calls Christ a “pattern” (typos, 1 Peter 5:3; cf. 1 Peter 2:21). Gill observes that Christ is an example to his people “in the exercise of grace — of meekness, humility, love, patience” — not in his miracles or his mediatorial work, which none can imitate.

Calvin, for the Reformed, guards the principle carefully: “we are not at liberty to take all his actions, without reserve, as subjects of imitation.” He turns it against those who, “by Christ’s example,” keep the forty days’ fast of Lent, noting that Christ never “intended to lay down his fast as an example… as a rule.” The witnesses thus find the verse teaching a discriminating discipleship. Christ is the believer’s pattern, but the pattern is the spirit of his action, not its outward form: we are to copy his humility and love, not to mimic each particular deed. The foot-washing is given “as an example” precisely that its lesson — the stooping of love to serve — may be carried into every circumstance, washed feet or no. To do “as” he did is to bear the same mind of self-forgetting service toward one another that moved the Lord to take the basin and the towel.

The witnesses add that this discriminating view of imitation preserves both the force and the freedom of Christ’s example. We are bound to copy his humility and love absolutely; we are not bound to reproduce the outward circumstance of a basin and a towel. So the command neither shrinks into a mere ceremony nor evaporates into a vague sentiment, but lays on every disciple the obligation to render, in his own time and place, the same self-forgetting service his Lord rendered at the supper.
16:
“Verily, verily, I say unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord; neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him.” The witnesses take this solemn maxim as the clinching argument for humility. The grammatical-historical commentators note the proverb, used elsewhere in the Gospels (Matthew 10:24) and again in this one (John 15:20): Robertson marks the comparative “not greater” setting slave against lord and “he that is sent” (the apostle) against “he that sent him.” Clarke would render the second clause “nor an apostle,” taking it as aimed “for the suppression of all worldly ambition and lordly conduct in the apostles and their successors in the ministry.” Barnes adds that the disciples “were to manifest the same spirit that he did, and… to expect the same treatment from the world” (Matthew 10:25).

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, sums the whole scene: Christ “said this… because He had washed the disciples’ feet, as the Master of humility both by word and example.” The logic the witnesses draw is plain and inescapable. If the Lord washes his servants’ feet, no servant may think such service beneath him; if the Sender stoops, the sent may not stand on their dignity. The argument runs the other way from the world’s: where men reason that the greater need not serve the less, Christ reasons that since the greatest has served, none of the lesser may refuse. The witnesses thus find in the verse the death-blow to all pride of place among Christ’s people: the disciple who claims exemption from lowly service claims to be greater than his Lord — an absurdity that the foot-washing has just exposed. Humility is not optional for the followers of a Master who knelt at their feet.

The witnesses observe how the proverb cuts at the root of all ecclesiastical pride. If even the apostles, the sent ones, are not above their Sender, then no minister or office-bearer in the church may lord it over the flock or count any service beneath his rank. The verse thus stands as a perpetual rebuke to ambition in Christ’s ministers, recalling them to the towel rather than the throne, and to the mind of the Master who washed his servants’ feet.
17:
“If ye know these things, happy are ye if ye do them.” The witnesses press the gap between knowing and doing, and the blessedness that lies only in the latter. The grammatical-historical commentators note the conditional structure — Robertson marks the two clauses, knowing and doing — and the point that mere knowledge confers no blessing. Ellicott: the blessedness is “in the doing, not in the knowing.” Barnes and Gill draw the practical lesson that to understand Christ’s teaching on humility and yet not practise it is to forfeit the happiness it promises; the beatitude is reserved for those who carry the lesson into life. The verse, several observe, gently warns the disciples (and every reader) against the deceit of supposing that to grasp a duty is to have discharged it.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here a word that searches all religion of mere profession. It is possible to know “these things” — the duty of humble, mutual love — clearly and even to admire them, while remaining proud and unserving; and such knowledge, far from blessing, increases responsibility. The happiness Christ pronounces is not on the learned but on the obedient: “blessed shall ye be if ye do them” (Augustine, at v.16). The witnesses thus close the foot-washing discourse on its sharpest edge. Christ has given the act, accepted his titles, drawn the lesson, and sealed it with a proverb; now he lays the whole upon the conscience. To know the way of humble love is good; to walk in it is blessed. The disciple is left not with a doctrine to admire but a duty to do — and the promise that in the doing, not the knowing, lies the joy of the servants who follow their Lord to the basin and the towel.

The witnesses draw out the warning latent in the words. To know a duty and not do it is not a neutral thing; it deepens guilt and forfeits blessing. The very clearness with which Christ has set humility before them leaves the disciples without excuse if they fail to practise it. So the verse turns the whole foot-washing from a scene to be admired into a command to be obeyed, and pronounces its blessing only on those who, having seen and understood, go and do likewise.
18:
“I speak not of you all: I know whom I have chosen: but that the scripture may be fulfilled, He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me.” The witnesses note the dark turn back to the betrayer amid the beatitude of v.17. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that when Christ pronounced the disciples “clean” and “happy,” he excepts one: “I speak not of you all.” The phrase “whom I have chosen” they take of the choice to apostleship (Barnes; cf. John 6:70; Luke 6:13), since Judas was “chosen” to the office though never cleansed in heart. Christ traces the treachery to Scripture — “He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me” (Psalm 41:9) — the bitter image of a table-companion turning to kick like a vicious beast. Ellicott notes the emphatic “I know,” and that nothing in the betrayal takes Christ by surprise.

The Eastern Chrysostom dwells on the horror of Judas’s fall: “a grievous thing it is to come to the depths of wickedness, for then the soul becomes hard to be restored,” and he marvels that Judas, “enjoying great assistance,” fell nonetheless. Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out John’s pastoral purpose: Christ declares “that there is one among the disciples who… is the very reverse of a disciple,” partly “for the sake of Judas, in order to render him the more inexcusable,” and partly for the others, “that they may not be overpowered by the ruin of Judas.” The witnesses thus find the verse both a warning and a comfort. The betrayal is no accident but the fulfilment of prophecy, foreseen and chosen-into by the Lord who reads every heart; and the falling away of one of the twelve, far from shaking the faith of the rest, is set before them as a thing written long before, that they may not stumble when treachery appears even at the table of the Lord.

The witnesses note the poignancy of the Psalm Christ chooses. ‘He that eateth bread with me’ marks the betrayer as a table-companion, one bound by the sacred ties of shared food and trust; to lift the heel against such a friend is the basest treachery. That Christ should apply these words at the very supper, having just washed the traitor’s feet, deepens the wonder of his patience — he names the betrayal even as he serves the betrayer, and reads it as the fulfilment of a Scripture written a thousand years before.
19:
“Now I tell you before it come, that, when it is come to pass, ye may believe that I am he.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s purpose in foretelling the betrayal — the confirming of his disciples’ faith. The grammatical-historical commentators note the structure: he tells them “from henceforth” (ap’ arti, Robertson) “before it come to pass,” so that “when it does come to pass” they “may believe that I am he.” Barnes explains the force: by the fulfilment “they would see that he had a knowledge of the heart and the power of foretelling future events, and must therefore have been sent by God”; this does not imply they had no faith before, “but that their faith would be increased and strengthened.” Clarke agrees these “frequent predictions of his death, so circumstantial,” tended “to confirm the disciples… in the belief of his being the Messiah, but also in that of his omniscience.” Ellicott connects the great “I am he” with the divine self-designation (John 8:24; cf. John 14:29).

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the same comfort more broadly. By this statement Christ “reminds his disciples that, when one of their number becomes a reprobate, this is so far from being a good reason for their being discouraged, that it ought to be a more full confirmation of their faith.” For “if we did not see before our eyes, in the Church, what has been foretold about her distresses and struggles, a doubt might justly arise… Where are the prophecies? But when the truth of Scripture agrees with our daily experience,” faith is the more established. The witnesses thus find the verse turning the scandal of betrayal into a buttress of belief. The very thing that might have shaken the disciples — that one of the twelve should prove a traitor — is foretold precisely so that, when it happens, it may prove rather than disprove their Master: he who knew the heart of Judas and named the deed beforehand is shown thereby to be the omniscient Son of God, “I am he.”
20:
“Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that receiveth whomsoever I send receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me.” The witnesses note that this saying, found also in the earlier Gospels (Matthew 10:40), is set here amid the dark word about the traitor. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the connection to the disciples’ mission. Ellicott: having spoken of their being sent as his servants, of the betrayal by one of their number, and of his foretelling it, Christ now bids them “go forth… and be content if their path is as that which their Master has trodden,” not “disheartened by treachery even in their midst.” The Eastern Chrysostom marvels at the dignity conferred: “what can be equal to the receiving Christ and His Father?” — for to welcome the apostle is to welcome the One who sent him, and through him the Father. Clarke reads it as comfort: though the disciples “should be rejected by many, they would be received by several,” and “whoever received them should reap the utmost benefit.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, judges that Christ here “intended to provide against this scandal” — the stumbling that Judas’s crime might cause. There is “too good evidence that we are very ready to be wounded” by the falls of those in office; so Christ assures the eleven that their ministry stands in unbroken union with himself and the Father, whatever one apostate may do. Wesley sums the dignity tersely: “I put my own honour upon you, my ambassadors.” The witnesses thus find the verse a deliberate encouragement set against the betrayal: lest the treachery of one should make the disciples doubt the worth of their calling, Christ exalts that calling to its height. The faithful messenger he sends carries Christ himself to those who receive him, and Christ carries the Father; so the sin of Judas cannot diminish the glory of the apostleship, nor the unbroken chain that binds the sent one to the Sender and to God.

The witnesses observe how tenderly Christ tends his disciples’ fears even while bearing his own. In the same breath that he speaks of betrayal he exalts their mission, lest the sin of one shake the confidence of the rest. It is the mark of his love that, troubled in spirit, he yet turns to comfort and dignify the faithful, binding them so closely to himself and the Father that no treachery can sever the bond. The apostate falls; the apostleship stands, carrying Christ and God to all who receive it.
21:
“When Jesus had thus said, he was troubled in spirit, and testified, and said, Verily, verily, I say unto you, that one of you shall betray me.” The witnesses dwell on the trouble of Christ and the open announcement. The Latin Augustine asks whether the betrayal “did this occur then for the first time to His mind,” and answers no: Christ “was now about to make the traitor known, and single him out from the rest, and therefore was troubled in spirit” — troubled “not in flesh, but in spirit,” by his own will, as before (John 11:33). The Eastern Chrysostom adds that, having twice promised help to the apostles in their future labours, Christ “remembers that the traitor is cut off from both, and is troubled at the thought.” The grammatical-historical commentators ground the trouble in his true humanity: Barnes says he was “deeply affected with the ingratitude and wickedness of a professed friend,” his “tender sensibilities… affected not less deeply than would be those of other men by baseness and treason”; Clarke notes he “testified” — “spoke with great earnestness” — and wonders whether even now Christ sought Judas’s benefit, that he might repent.

The witnesses mark the solemnity of the double “Verily, verily,” by which Christ now states plainly what he had only hinted: not merely that “ye are not all clean” (v.10), but “one of you shall betray me.” Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in his agitation the measure of the evil: the Lord of glory, untroubled by his own coming death, is moved to the depths by the treachery of one he had chosen, taught, and just now served. The witnesses thus read the verse as the gateway to the most painful scene of the supper. The love that washed the feet of all, including the betrayer, now feels the wound of betrayal, and out of a troubled spirit speaks the word that will sift the twelve — that one who has eaten his bread and worn his trust is about to deliver him to death.

The witnesses note that the announcement is also a final appeal. By declaring openly that one of them would betray him, Christ gives Judas a last opportunity to relent, naming the sin before it is done. The troubled spirit of the Lord is thus no mere foreknowledge of pain, but the grief of love over a soul rushing to ruin. Even here, at the threshold of the betrayal, the door of repentance is held open; and the very plainness of the warning leaves the traitor without excuse.
22:
“Then the disciples looked one on another, doubting of whom he spake.” The witnesses picture the bewilderment that swept the table at Christ’s words. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the announcement caught the eleven wholly by surprise: though Christ had often foretold his death, none of them suspected that one of their own number could be the traitor (Henry), so they “looked one on another,” perplexed and uncertain “of whom he spake” (Gill, Barnes). Robertson and Ellicott observe that the glances around the table betray both their innocence and their alarm — each searching the others’ faces, and (the Synoptics add) each asking, “Lord, is it I?”, more ready to doubt himself than to accuse a brother. The very confusion testifies that Judas had so well concealed his purpose that not one disciple could name him.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the scene both a sober warning and a quiet commendation. The disciples’ inability to detect the traitor shows how deep hypocrisy may lie hidden, even in the closest fellowship, undiscerned by the holiest company; and their readiness to suspect themselves rather than each other is the mark of humble and tender consciences. The witnesses thus read the verse as the hush of dismay before the unmasking. The word of Christ has fallen like a stone into still water; the disciples, troubled and searching, cannot tell on whom it lights, and their perplexity prepares the way for the question that John, leaning on the Master’s breast, will be moved to ask — “Lord, who is it?” The traitor is among them, known to Christ alone, and the others can only look from face to face in grief and fear.

The witnesses draw the lesson that none should be hasty to think himself secure. That the disciples asked ‘Lord, is it I?’ rather than pointing at a neighbour shows a wholesome distrust of their own hearts, fitting for all who would follow Christ. Yet the scene also warns that outward fellowship is no guarantee of inward truth: a traitor sat among the twelve, undetected by the rest, sharing the bread and the basin. The same word that comforts the faithful searches every conscience at the table.
23:
“Now there was leaning on Jesus’ bosom one of his disciples, whom Jesus loved.” The witnesses identify this disciple and dwell on the intimacy the posture expresses. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the reclining custom: at the meal the guests lay on couches, propped on the left arm, so that the one next to Jesus would naturally rest, when he turned, against his breast (Gill, Lightfoot, Robertson). The unnamed “disciple whom Jesus loved” is, all agree, John himself, the Evangelist, who throughout his Gospel veils his name under this loving title — a touch of modesty that Augustine notes is John’s own self-designation. Robertson marks the phrase “on Jesus’ bosom” (en tō kolpō) as the place of nearest fellowship at the table.

The Latin fathers read the posture as a figure of deeper things. Bede says that John’s lying “in the bosom, and upon the breast, was not only an evidence of present love, but also a sign of the future” — of “those new and mysterious doctrines which he was afterwards commissioned to reveal to the world.” Augustine asks, “For by bosom what else is signified but secret?” — “the hollow of the breast, the secret chamber of wisdom,” so that the disciple who leaned there became the channel of the loftiest revelation, the eagle of the Gospel. The witnesses thus find the verse setting a tender scene of love against the dark announcement of betrayal: while the rest look round in dismay, the beloved disciple rests upon his Master’s breast, near enough to be asked, and to ask, the secret of the traitor. The nearness of love is what fits John both to learn the answer now and, in the fathers’ reading, to draw from that same breast the deep things of God he would later write.

The witnesses find in the beloved disciple’s posture a quiet emblem of the believer’s privilege. To lean on the breast of Christ is to be admitted to his closest confidence, near enough to hear the secrets of his heart. John’s place at the table thus pictures the intimacy of love to which the gospel calls every disciple, and from which flow both the knowledge of Christ’s mind and the courage to ask him what we long to know. The nearer the heart rests on Christ, the more freely it may inquire of him.
24:
“Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him, that he should ask who it should be of whom he spake.” The witnesses picture the impulsive Peter prompting the beloved disciple to put the question. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Peter, reclining at some distance from Jesus and unable to whisper to him directly, “beckoned” to John (the present tense, Ellicott, “Simon Peter beckons”), who lay nearest the Master. Gill explains that such silent signs were “usual at meals, when they could not, by reason of their posture, discourse together.” Robertson glosses the verb (neuei, to nod) and reads Peter’s character: “cautious, but could not contain his curiosity,” and so he turns to John, who, “in front of Jesus, was in a favourable position to have a whispered word with him.” Ellicott recalls that Peter and John, both once disciples of the Baptist (John 1:40–41), were lifelong companions (cf. Acts 3:1), so it is natural that Peter should make his appeal to John.

The witnesses find in the little scene a vivid touch of eye-witness truth, and a study in the two apostles’ temperaments. Peter, ever forward, must know at once who the traitor is, yet defers to John’s nearness; John, resting on the Master’s breast, is the one fitted to ask. The verse thus shows the company in their confusion (v.22) turning to the disciple of nearest intimacy to draw out the secret. The witnesses read it as a small parable of access: it is the one who leans on Christ’s bosom who is best placed to learn his mind, and through whom the rest may have their question answered. Peter’s sign and John’s leaning together prepare the quiet, whispered exchange by which the betrayer will be made known to one, though hidden still from the rest.

The witnesses note that Peter, for all his boldness, here defers — unable to reach Jesus himself, he works through the disciple who lies nearer. The little arrangement shows how the company instinctively turned to John as the one closest to the Master’s heart. It is a quiet illustration of the body of Christ at work: each member using his own place and gift, the bold prompting, the beloved asking, that the truth they all desire may be drawn from the Lord.
25:
“He then lying on Jesus’ breast saith unto him, Lord, who is it?” The witnesses note the change of posture by which John puts the whispered question. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the word here for “lying” differs from “leaning” in v.23: Barnes explains it means “falling back or laid his head back on the bosom of Jesus, so that he could speak to him privately without being heard by the others,” and Robertson marks the participle (anapesōn, to fall back) with the “thus” of vivid description — “it was easily done.” Clarke infers from the secrecy that “John altered his position at table, in order to ask the question,” which he did “by whispering,” for the other disciples “had not heard what John said” (as v.28 shows). Wesley sums it: “leaning down, and so asking him privately.”

The witnesses dwell on the intimacy and the reverence the scene displays. John does not call out the question across the table, nor demand the answer aloud, but draws close to the Master’s breast and asks softly, “Lord, who is it?” The address “Lord” keeps the reverence even in the closeness; the whisper keeps the secret even in the asking. The witnesses thus find in the verse a model of how the believer may inquire of Christ — near enough for confidence, lowly enough for awe. The beloved disciple, resting on the bosom that is “the secret chamber of wisdom,” asks the one thing the troubled company longs to know, and asks it in the posture of love and trust. His private question will draw a private answer, by which the traitor is shown to one who can be trusted to keep it, until the deed itself reveals what the sign had quietly disclosed.

The witnesses observe that the whole exchange takes place in the closest possible nearness, breast to breast, voice to ear. The secret of the betrayer is too weighty to be cried aloud, and is entrusted only to the disciple of nearest love. So the verse teaches that the deepest confidences of Christ are shared with those who lie nearest him; the heart that rests on his breast is the heart to which he discloses what he hides from the crowd.
26:
“Jesus answered, He it is, to whom I shall give a sop, when I have dipped it. And when he had dipped the sop, he gave it to Judas Iscariot.” The witnesses dwell on the sign, its secrecy, and its tenderness. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the “sop” (psōmion, a morsel of bread, only here in the New Testament) dipped in the dish — “a token of intimacy to allow a guest to dip his bread in the common dish” (Robertson; cf. Ruth 2:14; Mark 14:20). Lightfoot and Clarke note that to dip and hand a sop was “a very unusual thing,” so the other disciples likely took it as a mark of favour, or as Christ sending Judas on some errand, and so missed its meaning. Wesley draws out the pathos: Jesus answered John “in his ear, so careful was he not to offend (if it had been possible) even Judas himself,” and “probably the other disciples thought Judas peculiarly happy.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, anticipates the objection that Satan should enter Judas after receiving Christ’s bread — “Was this the worth of Christ’s bread?” — and answers that the morsel was not poison but a sign, the guilt lying in the heart that received love while plotting murder. Calvin, for the Reformed, asks why a sign was used when Christ might have named the traitor openly, and answers that “the sign was of such a nature, that it discovered Judas to one person only,” so that John might be a “witness of this fact” to reveal “at the proper time,” while Christ “intentionally delayed” the public exposure. The witnesses thus find the sop a marvel of love and restraint: the Lord points out his betrayer not with an accusing word but with a gift, handing the morsel of friendship to the very man about to sell him, and revealing the secret only to the disciple who could keep it. The last token Judas receives from Christ is a morsel of bread from his hand.

The witnesses find in the sop a final, wordless plea. By handing Judas the morsel of friendship, Christ appeals once more to a conscience already sold, offering the token of fellowship to the very man who would sell him. That the appeal is refused makes the next moment the darker; but that it is made at all is the measure of a love that, having washed the traitor’s feet, now feeds him from its own hand. The last thing Christ does for Judas is an act of kindness.
27:
“And after the sop Satan entered into him. Then said Jesus unto him, That thou doest, do quickly.” The witnesses mark the decisive moment of Judas’s surrender to Satan and the meaning of Christ’s words. The grammatical-historical commentators note that this is “the only time the word Satan occurs in the Gospel” (Robertson), and that, the devil having “before this put it into his heart” (v.2; Luke 22:3), he now “entered” to confirm and hurry the deed. Ellicott catches the awful instant: “it was then, when the heart hardened itself to receive from Jesus the sacred pledge of love, while it was plotting in black hatred how to betray Him,” that Satan took full possession. Barnes adds that, seeing Jesus knew his plan and that the others would soon know it, Judas, “aroused by sudden anger,” resolved “on executing his plan at once.” Lightfoot reads the deeper type: as Satan “had entered into the serpent that deceived the first Adam, so… the second Adam could not be betrayed but by one into whom he should first enter.”

On “That thou doest, do quickly,” the witnesses are careful about its force. It is not, Wesley insists, “a permission, much less a command,” but as if Christ had said, “if thou art determined to do it, why dost thou delay?” — showing Judas “that he could not be hid,” and “expressing his own readiness to suffer.” Clarke agrees: Judas was “past all counsel,” and Christ, master of his own hour, would not have the dark business drawn out. The witnesses also reject any notion that the sop was the Lord’s Supper — “this is an utter mistake” (Clarke). The witnesses thus find the verse the point of no return: love’s last appeal, the morsel from Christ’s hand, only hardens the traitor, and Satan enters where grace is finally refused. Christ’s calm “do quickly” is the word of one who, far from being overtaken by events, sets the very hour of his betrayal, going to his death not as a victim of Judas but as the Lord who lays down his life.
28:
“Now no man at the table knew for what intent he spake this unto him.” The witnesses take this as the Evangelist’s own recollection of the company’s ignorance. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the sign and the whispered word had revealed the traitor to John alone, so that the rest, hearing only “That thou doest, do quickly,” did not connect it with the betrayal (Gill, Barnes). Ellicott reads it as “a comment of the Apostle’s, as he writes in remembrance of the impression made at the time”: they heard the words to Judas, “but none of them knew until afterwards that these words referred to the betrayal.” Robertson sums it: “the disciples had not yet perceived the treacherous heart of Judas.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs why even John’s knowledge did not spread: either “John had not yet related to others what Christ had told him,” or the disciples “were so much struck by it, that they lost their presence of mind” — indeed, “it is probable that John himself was almost out of his senses.” And he draws the abiding lesson: “what then happened to the disciples we frequently see taking place in the Church, that few of the believers discern the hypocrites whom the Lord loudly condemns.” The witnesses thus find in this verse a sober commentary on the hiddenness of the traitor. Even at the moment of his unmasking, Judas passed unsuspected by the eleven; the sign meant for John meant nothing to them. So the church is warned that the false brother may sit undetected at the holiest table, known to Christ and perhaps to one or two, yet hidden from the rest — and that the want of discernment among the godly is no new thing, but was true even of the apostles at the Supper.

The witnesses draw the warning that nearness to Christ and his ordinances is no proof of a true heart. The eleven sat at the same table as the traitor and could not tell him from themselves; the false brother was hidden in the holiest fellowship. So the verse cautions the church against presuming on outward communion, and reminds it that the searching knowledge of hearts belongs to Christ alone, who saw in Judas what no disciple discerned.
29:
“For some of them thought, because Judas had the bag, that Jesus had said unto him, Buy those things that we have need of against the feast; or, that he should give something to the poor.” The witnesses explain the disciples’ innocent misreading of Christ’s command. The grammatical-historical commentators recall that Judas “had the bag,” the common purse of the company (Gill, Barnes; cf. John 12:6), so that when Christ said “do quickly,” the others naturally supposed he was sending the treasurer on some ordinary errand — to “buy those things… against the feast,” or to “give something to the poor,” a customary act of charity at the Passover season. Ellicott and Robertson note how exactly this fits the office Judas held; the very thing that made him the thief made him the natural one to send on such business.

Clarke adds a chronological observation from Calmet: since the disciples thought Judas was sent to “buy” for the feast, and “on the day of the passover there was neither buying nor selling in Jerusalem,” this supper cannot have been the Passover meal itself but the evening before — a point bearing on the harmony of the Gospels. The witnesses, however, chiefly dwell on the irony the verse contains. The eleven, suspecting nothing, imagine their fellow on an errand of provision or mercy, when in truth he is going out to sell their Master for thirty pieces of silver. The bag that marked him as trusted steward becomes the cover of his treachery; the very innocence of the disciples’ guesses measures how completely Judas had deceived them. The witnesses thus find the verse heightening the pathos of the scene: love and charity are imagined where betrayal is afoot, and the traitor passes from the table of his Lord under the guise of a servant on holy business.

The witnesses note the deep irony that the disciples imagined an errand of provision or charity at the very moment of betrayal. Their innocent guesses measure how thoroughly Judas had concealed his treachery behind the office of a trusted steward. The bag that should have served the poor became the cover of the deed that sold the Master; and the company, suspecting nothing, let the traitor go out with their blessing on what they took to be holy business.
30:
“He then having received the sop went immediately out: and it was night.” The witnesses note the abrupt departure and the heavy closing words. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the haste — Judas “went immediately out” (euthys, Robertson), the deed now set in motion — leaving the supper to do the work Satan had confirmed in him. But it is the final clause that the witnesses find most pregnant: “and it was night.” On the surface it is a simple note of time, fixing the hour of Judas’s going; yet the Evangelist, Robertson and Ellicott agree, surely intends a deeper sense — the symbolic darkness into which the traitor passed. He went out from the light of the world (John 8:12) into the night, both of the hour and of his own soul.

The witnesses dwell on the solemn contrast the verse draws. Within, the light still shone, the love still spoke, the Master still taught; without lay the night, into which Judas plunged, having refused the last morsel of grace. “It was night,” the witnesses say, in every sense: the night of the world’s darkest deed, the night of the betrayer’s lost soul, the night before the cross. The going-out of Judas is thus the going-out of one who leaves the fellowship of Christ for the darkness beyond it — a darkness he chose when he received the sop and rose from the table. The witnesses find in these three words a whole theology of apostasy: to turn from Christ is to go out into the night, and there is no darker night than that of a soul that has sat at the Lord’s table, been served by his hands, and gone out to betray him. With Judas gone, the chapter turns to the light that remains, and to the new commandment Christ will give those who stay.

The witnesses observe that the departure of Judas clears the room for the discourse that follows. Once the traitor is gone out into the night, Christ turns to the eleven who remain and opens to them, in the verses ahead, the new commandment and the promises of the upper room. So the going-out of the one who chose darkness becomes the threshold of the brightest teaching of the supper, given to those who stayed in the light of their Lord.
31:
“Therefore, when he was gone out, Jesus said, Now is the Son of man glorified, and God is glorified in him.” The witnesses note that the departure of Judas opens the way for the great discourse of the upper room, and that Christ speaks of his coming death as glory. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the “now”: with the betrayer gone, “the last deed is done that was necessary to secure the death of the Son of man” (Barnes), and Christ sees in that death “the glory of His accomplished work… as present” (Ellicott; cf. John 12:23). Robertson reads “now at last, the crisis has come,” the aorist “is glorified” marking “the consummation of glory in death both for the Son and the Father,” the verb sounding four times in these verses. Clarke paraphrases: “now it fully appears that I am the person appointed to redeem a lost world by my blood.” Henry notes the turn to “table-talk”: Judas gone, the Master and the eleven “applied themselves to profitable discourse.”

The Eastern Origen reads the going-out of Judas as the threshold of the cross’s glory: “Christ at His death, in which He glorified God, having spoiled principalities and powers,” entered his glorifying. Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the consolation for the disciples: “the last hour was at hand,” and Christ, knowing “the minds of his disciples were very weak,” supports them by showing “that he triumphed in the cross, having obtained a victory over Satan, sin, and death.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the paradox at the heart of the Passion: that the cross, the world’s deepest shame, is the Son of man’s glory and the Father’s. The betrayal now set in motion is not the defeat of Christ but the means of his glorifying; and he speaks of it as already accomplished, so sure is the victory, so bright the glory that shines through the darkness of the path that leads to it.

The witnesses note how the verse reframes the whole Passion. To the eye of the world, what is about to happen is the disgraceful death of a condemned man; to Christ it is glory — his own and his Father’s. By naming the cross ‘glory’ before it comes, he teaches his disciples, and the church after them, to read his sufferings not as defeat but as the supreme manifestation of God. The darkest hour of history is, in his own reckoning, the hour of glory.
32:
“If God be glorified in him, God shall also glorify him in himself, and shall straightway glorify him.” The witnesses unfold the mutual glory of Father and Son and the nearness of its accomplishment. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the movement from the aorist of v.31 to the future here: if God is glorified in the Son’s death, “God shall also glorify him” — Ellicott notes “the tense now changes to the future, and the glory thought of is that of the Father’s throne,” the phrase “in himself” referring to God, who is “the source of the glory” (Robertson; cf. John 17:5). Barnes reads it as Christ’s confident assurance that “God will honour the Messiah… will not suffer him to go without a proper attestation of his acceptance.” On “straightway,” Robertson marks “no postponement now” — “first and quickly the Cross, then the Ascension” — while Clarke finds an earnest of it even in the Passion, as when the band that came to seize him “fell to the ground” (John 18:6).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the unity of purpose: Christ “concludes that he will obtain a triumph by his death, because his sole design in it is to glorify his Father; for the Father did not seek his glory from the death of his Son in such a manner as not to make the Son a partaker of that glory.” So “when the ignominy which he shall endure for a short time has been effaced, illustrious honour will be displayed in his death.” The witnesses thus find in this verse the reciprocity of glory that runs through John’s Gospel: the Son glorifies the Father in the cross, and the Father glorifies the Son in himself, exalting him to the throne of heaven. And the “straightway” presses the imminence — the glory is not a distant hope but the immediate sequel of the suffering now at hand, so that the disciples, soon to see only shame, are told that beyond it, and quickly, lies the Son’s glorification by God himself.

The witnesses draw the comfort for the trembling disciples. Christ does not leave the cross to stand alone in their minds, but binds it at once to the glory that follows ‘straightway.’ They are to look through the shame to the exaltation, assured that the Father who is glorified in the Son’s death will quickly glorify the Son in heaven. So the verse steadies faith against the coming offence of the cross, fixing the eye on the throne beyond it.
33:
“Little children, yet a little while I am with you. Ye shall seek me: and as I said unto the Jews, Whither I go, ye cannot come; so now I say to you.” The witnesses are touched by the tender address and the warning of departure. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on “little children” (teknia): Robertson notes the affectionate diminutive, used by Jesus “only here in this Gospel,” but “common in I John” (1 John 2:1), as if the word so impressed John that he made it his own. Clarke renders it “beloved children,” “such as a fond mother uses to her most beloved babes,” and observes that “now that Judas was gone out, he could use this epithet without restriction.” Ellicott hears in it the thought of their coming “orphanage” when he departs. On “whither I go, ye cannot come,” several note Christ repeats to the disciples what he had said to the hostile Jews (John 7:33; 8:21), but in love, not judgment — Wesley adds “not yet; being not yet ripe for it.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, guards the saying from misunderstanding: lest they think he would “straightway” be so glorified as to have no more converse with them, he says “yet a little while I am with you,” for after his resurrection “He was with them forty days” before ascending. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the words as both warning and comfort: Christ “gives them early warning that he will no longer be with them, and… exhorts them to patience,” and “to remove unseasonable eagerness of desire, he declares that they cannot immediately follow him”; yet by the gentle name “little children” he shows “that his reason for departing is not that he cares little about their welfare.” The witnesses thus find the verse a model of how Christ prepares his own for loss: with tenderest affection he warns them of his going, restrains their impatience to follow, and so begins to wean them from his bodily presence toward the faith and love that must sustain them when he is gone. The same word that announces the parting cushions it with a father’s love.
34:
“A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.” The witnesses ask in what sense the commandment is “new,” since love of neighbour was already commanded (Leviticus 19:18). Augustine, for the Latin fathers, raises and answers the question: it is new because it renews us, dividing us from “the old man” and making us new. The grammatical-historical commentators locate the newness chiefly in the standard. Robertson notes the adjective “new” (kainēn) against the “old” of 1 John 2:7: “they had had it a long time, but the practice of it was new,” and the new measure is “as I have loved you.” Clarke presses this: under Moses a man should love his neighbour “as himself,” but Christ “more than fulfilled the Mosaic precept; he not only loved his neighbour as himself, but… more than himself, for he laid down his life.” Barnes calls it new because given as “a badge of discipleship,” by which they should be “distinguished from all others,” and Ellicott sets the contrast not against the Decalogue but against what Christ had said to the unbelieving Jews — to the disciples he gives the law of mutual love.

The witnesses thus find the commandment new in degree and pattern though old in substance. The old law bade men love their neighbour as themselves; the new bids the disciples love one another as Christ has loved them — with a love that stoops to wash feet and lays down its life. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as the exhortation that crowns the consolation: “while I am absent from you in body, testify, by mutual love, that I have not taught you in vain.” The witnesses find here the abiding rule of the Christian fellowship: not merely a kindly regard among brethren, but a love measured by the cross — self-giving, serving, sacrificial — which is to be the very life of the church Christ leaves behind. The new commandment is the law of the new covenant, and its standard is the love of the Lord who gave it.
35:
“By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another.” The witnesses make this the badge and proof of discipleship. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the mark by which the world is to recognise Christ’s followers is not power, knowledge, or outward profession, but mutual love. Barnes: this love was given “to be a badge of discipleship, by which they might be known as his friends and followers, and… distinguished from all others.” Robertson notes “by this” (en toutō) as the test, and that it is love, not miracle or gift, that marks the disciple. Gill and Ellicott agree that the visible love of Christians for one another is the credential the world can read, the sign that they belong to the Master who loved them.

The witnesses dwell on the weight of this standard. Christ does not say the world will know his disciples by their orthodoxy, their zeal, or their success, but by their love — and love after the pattern just given, “as I have loved you” (v.34). Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here both a comfort and a rebuke to the church: a comfort, that the humblest fellowship marked by genuine love bears the surest evidence of belonging to Christ; a rebuke, wherever the followers of Christ are known to the world for anything sooner than their love for one another. The witnesses thus read the verse as the seal upon the new commandment: the love of the brethren is not a private virtue only but a public testimony, the visible argument by which all men may see that these are indeed the disciples of the One who loved them to the end. Where that love is, the world has proof; where it is wanting, the profession is empty.

The witnesses press the practical weight of the test. It is a solemn thing that Christ should hang the world’s recognition of his people upon their love for one another. Doctrine may be sound and zeal great, yet if love be absent the credential is wanting; and where love abounds, the humblest fellowship carries the seal of Christ. The verse thus lays upon every age of the church the charge to make love its mark, that the world, reading it, may know whose disciples they are.
36:
“Simon Peter said unto him, Lord, whither goest thou? Jesus answered him, Whither I go, thou canst not follow me now; but thou shalt follow me afterwards.” The witnesses note Peter’s love-driven curiosity and the promise hidden in Christ’s reply. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that Peter, fastening on the words “whither I go, ye cannot come” (v.33), cannot bear to be parted from his Master and presses, “Lord, whither goest thou?” The Eastern Chrysostom reads in it “the great love” of Peter, who “could not endure” the thought of separation. Christ’s answer distinguishes “now” from “afterwards”: Peter “canst not follow now” — he is not yet ready, and the way of the cross is Christ’s alone to tread first — “but thou shalt follow me afterwards.” The Latin Augustine and the commentators agree that the “afterwards” points to Peter’s own martyrdom, when he too would lay down his life for Christ and so follow him in death (cf. John 21:18–19).

The witnesses find in the exchange both gentleness and prophecy.
