Christ does not rebuke Peter’s desire to follow, but tempers it: the time is not yet, and the disciple who is so sure of himself must first pass through the failure that will teach him his weakness before he is fit to follow his Lord to the cross. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the tenderness in the deferral — the “afterwards” is a promise, not a refusal — and the wisdom that restrains Peter’s untimely ardour. The witnesses thus read the verse as the opening of the dialogue that will end in the foretelling of the denial. Peter, all eagerness to follow, is told he cannot yet; the path he would tread at once will indeed be his, but only after he has learned, through bitter falling, that the following of Christ is not in the strength of his own resolve but in the grace that will one day make even him faithful unto death.

The witnesses note the kindness folded into the deferral. Christ does not say Peter shall never follow, but only not yet; the way of the cross is closed to him for the moment, not forever. So the verse holds together a present restraint and a future promise, teaching the disciple to accept the limits Christ sets, and to trust that the path he longs to tread will be opened in its time, when he is made ready by grace.
37:
“Peter said unto him, Lord, why cannot I follow thee now? I will lay down my life for thy sake.” The witnesses hear in this the voice of Peter’s sincere but presumptuous self-confidence. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Peter, not content to wait for the “afterwards,” demands the “now,” and pledges the very thing Christ had said he could not yet do — to lay down his life. His love is genuine, all agree, but it overrates his own strength: he is sure that his devotion can carry him through any danger, and offers to die for his Master that very night. Robertson and Ellicott mark the impulsive boldness so characteristic of Peter, who speaks from the heart but without self-knowledge, ignorant of how soon and how completely his resolve will fail.

Calvin, for the Reformed, names the fault precisely: Peter “trusts too much to his own strength,” and in his confidence forgets that fidelity is not in the power of the flesh but the gift of grace. The very vehemence of his promise — “I will lay down my life for thy sake” — is the measure of his blindness to his own weakness. The witnesses thus find in the verse a mirror for every believer who is confident in his own steadfastness. Peter means what he says; his love is real; yet he reckons without the frailty of his nature and the fierceness of the coming trial. His boast sets up the solemn word of the next verse, in which Christ, who knows the heart better than its owner, foretells not the martyrdom Peter offers but the threefold denial he does not dream of. The disciple’s overconfidence is about to be answered by a prophecy of his fall.

The witnesses observe how little Peter knows himself. He measures his love by the strength of his feeling, and reckons himself able to die where he has not yet been tried. The verse thus stands as a caution to all earnest souls, who in the warmth of devotion promise more than their frailty can perform. True following waits on grace, not on the heat of resolve; and the disciple most sure of standing is often the nearest to a fall.
38:
“Jesus answered him, Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake? Verily, verily, I say unto thee, The cock shall not crow, till thou hast denied me thrice.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s gentle but searching exposure of Peter’s self-confidence. The grammatical-historical commentators note the form of the reply — Christ echoes Peter’s own words back as a question, “Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake?”, with a touch of sorrowful irony at the gap between the boast and the man. Then, with the solemn double “Verily,” he foretells the exact opposite of the martyrdom Peter offered: before the cock crows, “thou shalt deny me thrice.” Lightfoot and Robertson note the precision of the prophecy — the cock-crow, the threefold denial — which the night would fulfil to the letter. Christ, who reads the heart he had chosen, knows that the disciple so sure of dying for him will, within hours, three times deny that he even knows him.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the lesson against presumption: Peter’s confidence is rebuked not to crush him but to teach him, and through him the church, “how feeble and frail is the strength of men” when they lean on themselves. The very plainness of the prophecy is mercy: it will, when fulfilled, both humble Peter and, by its fulfilment, confirm his faith in the Christ who foretold it (cf. v.19). The witnesses thus close the chapter on a note of sober realism. The supper that began with the washing of feet and rose to the new commandment of love ends with the foretelling of a disciple’s fall — a reminder that even the most ardent love, trusting in its own strength, may fail, and that the following of Christ which Peter so boldly claimed must be learned through the breaking of his self-reliance. Yet the “afterwards” of v.36 still stands: the denier will one day, by grace, become the martyr, and follow his Lord at last.

The witnesses find in the close of the chapter a profound tenderness beneath the warning. Christ foretells the denial not in anger but in love, that Peter, when he has fallen and wept, may remember the word and be restored rather than destroyed. The same Lord who exposes the weakness has already prayed that his faith fail not, and has promised that he shall follow afterwards. So the prophecy of the fall is wrapped in the assurance of recovery, and the chapter that opened with love to the end closes with a love that will not let even the denier go.
